channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: An unusual strategy helps some tropical butterflies live 25 times longer than their relatives - KSL News
item.description:
1. An unusual strategy helps some tropical butterflies live 25 times longer than their relatives  KSL News
  2. Butterflies may hold clues to longer lives  NPR
  3. Evolution of increased longevity and slowed ageing in a genus of tropical butterfly  Nature
  4. Some tropical butterfly species have unlocked an evolutionary way to live longer  CNN
  5. The Butterfly Longevity Diet  The Atlantic

item.title: Scientists Just Found Something Weird Inside Moss - SciTechDaily
item.description:
1. Scientists Just Found Something Weird Inside Moss  SciTechDaily
  2. In a first, scientists find evidence of fungi hiding inside moss cells  Interesting Engineering
  3. Desert moss may hide one of Earth's oldest partnerships  Earth.com
  4. wet and dry moss (IMAGE)  EurekAlert!
  5. Hidden fungus inside desert moss could rewrite 470-million-year story of how plants moved onto land  Phys.org

item.title: Antarctica is offering 30 to 50 years' worth of warning on sea level rise, models suggest - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Antarctica is offering 30 to 50 years' worth of warning on sea level rise, models suggest  Phys.org
  2. Scientists say the West Antarctic Ice Sheet could collapse with very little additional warming and the four metres of sea level rise that would follow cannot be stopped once it begins  Space Daily
  3. Antarctica’s 50-year warning: Scientists say the next few years will shape the future of the West Antarctic Ice Sheet  The Times of India
  4. Vanderford Glacier, Credit: Monash SAEF (IMAGE)  EurekAlert!
  5. Emergent decadal predictability in Antarctic contribution to sea-level rise  Nature

item.title: No spacecraft has ever landed in the outer solar system — except one: the Huygens probe, which parachuted through Titan's orange haze in 2005 and touched down more than a billion kilometres from Earth in cold that dropped below minus 170 degrees Cels - Space Daily
item.description: No spacecraft has ever landed in the outer solar system — except one: the Huygens probe, which parachuted through Titan's orange haze in 2005 and touched down more than a billion kilometres from Earth in cold that dropped below minus 170 degrees Cels  Space Daily

item.title: A mysterious gamma-ray stream comes from the Milky Way's center. Could dark matter have something to do with it? - Space
item.description:
1. A mysterious gamma-ray stream comes from the Milky Way's center. Could dark matter have something to do with it?  Space
  2. Dark Matter in the Center of the Milky Way Not Ruled Out | Newswise  Newswise
  3. A strange glow in the heart of the Milky Way may be dark matter  The Brighter Side of News
  4. Dark matter cannot be ruled out as cause of gamma ray glow at the Milky Way's center, machine learning shows  Phys.org

item.title: There's an Astounding Amount of Fungi Beneath Our Feet - Newser
item.description:
1. There's an Astounding Amount of Fungi Beneath Our Feet  Newser
  2. Our Hidden Fungal Networks Could Reach Beyond the Solar System  Mother Jones
  3. A Hidden Universe: Underground Fungi Could Stretch to the Sun Nearly a Billion Times  atmos.earth
  4. Nearly All Plants Depend on Tiny Underground Fungi. The Microbes' Vast Global Networks Were Just Mapped for the First Time  Smithsonian Magazine
  5. There’s an Unfathomably Huge Network of Fungi Lurking Beneath the Earth  VICE

item.title: Humans May Soon Be Able to Regrow Body Parts—Including Fingers and Limbs—Thanks to a Groundbreaking Serum - Yahoo
item.description:
1. Humans May Soon Be Able to Regrow Body Parts—Including Fingers and Limbs—Thanks to a Groundbreaking Serum  Yahoo

  3. "Once you show regeneration can be activated, it opens the door to new questions..." Mammals – including us – could regrow limbs say scientists  BBC Wildlife Magazine
  4. Mammal Tissue Regeneration Activated: FGF2 and BMP2 Override Scarring in Mice  Tech Times
  5. Scientists Discover Gene That One Day Could Help Humans Regrow Lost Limbs  ColombiaOne.com

item.title: On 5 March 1982, the Soviet Venera 14 lander reached the surface of Venus, ejected the protective cap from its camera lens, photographed the surrounding terrain — and then lowered its mechanical soil-testing arm directly onto the discarded lens cap, returni - 19FortyFive
item.description:
1. On 5 March 1982, the Soviet Venera 14 lander reached the surface of Venus, ejected the protective cap from its camera lens, photographed the surrounding terrain — and then lowered its mechanical soil-testing arm directly onto the discarded lens cap, returni  19FortyFive
  2. Venus’ Strange Rotation Was Likely Triggered By A High Velocity Moon-Sized Impactor  Universe Today

item.title: Our galaxy and its neighbours are racing through space at more than two million kilometres an hour, caught in a tug-of-war between the gravity of distant superclusters ahead and a vast, near-empty void shoving us from behind - Space Daily
item.description: Our galaxy and its neighbours are racing through space at more than two million kilometres an hour, caught in a tug-of-war between the gravity of distant superclusters ahead and a vast, near-empty void shoving us from behind  Space Daily

item.title: Thawing Permafrost Not Only Emits CO2, It Absorbs it Too, Shows Landmark Study - Good News Network
item.description:
1. Thawing Permafrost Not Only Emits CO2, It Absorbs it Too, Shows Landmark Study  Good News Network
  2. Rock weathering can counteract river CO2 emissions induced by permafrost thaw  Nature
  3. Earth’s permafrost could soon release hidden ‘deep carbon,’ supercharging warming  Yahoo
  4. How a new permafrost climate model fits a dangerous pattern in climate science  R&D World
  5. Northern permafrost switches from carbon sink to carbon source earlier than thought in models including deep soil carbon  Phys.org

item.title: NASA's STEREO-A spacecraft happened to be sitting in the path of the July 2012 Carrington-class storm and took the full hit instead of Earth — the only reason we have detailed measurements of a blow we never felt - Space Daily
item.description: NASA's STEREO-A spacecraft happened to be sitting in the path of the July 2012 Carrington-class storm and took the full hit instead of Earth — the only reason we have detailed measurements of a blow we never felt  Space Daily

item.title: It's Official: Third Galaxy Found With No Dark Matter May Solve an Epic Mystery - ScienceAlert
item.description:
1. It's Official: Third Galaxy Found With No Dark Matter May Solve an Epic Mystery  ScienceAlert

  4. Astronomers atop Maunakea discover third galaxy without dark matter  Maui Now
  5. Yale Astronomers Map Row Of Galaxies Lacking Dark Matter  Quantum Zeitgeist

item.title: Apollo 12 was struck by lightning twice in the first 52 seconds of flight, filling the spacecraft with warning lights and turning Mission Control’s data into nonsense — until a young controller named John Aaron recognised an obscure failure pattern and calmly sai - Space Daily
item.description: Apollo 12 was struck by lightning twice in the first 52 seconds of flight, filling the spacecraft with warning lights and turning Mission Control’s data into nonsense — until a young controller named John Aaron recognised an obscure failure pattern and calmly sai  Space Daily

item.title: NASA spent 14 years keeping the Galileo spacecraft alive through a jammed antenna and brutal radiation, then in 2003 deliberately flew it into Jupiter to be destroyed — not because it had failed, but because it had found a probable ocean on Europa it could n - 19FortyFive
item.description: NASA spent 14 years keeping the Galileo spacecraft alive through a jammed antenna and brutal radiation, then in 2003 deliberately flew it into Jupiter to be destroyed — not because it had failed, but because it had found a probable ocean on Europa it could n  19FortyFive

item.title: Voyager 1 launched in 1977 on a four-year mission and is still flying 49 years later, now so far away that in November 2026 a radio signal will take a full 24 hours to reach it — so when engineers say "good morning" on a Monday, the answer won't arrive until - 19FortyFive
item.description:
1. Voyager 1 launched in 1977 on a four-year mission and is still flying 49 years later, now so far away that in November 2026 a radio signal will take a full 24 hours to reach it — so when engineers say "good morning" on a Monday, the answer won't arrive until  19FortyFive
  2. It's Official: Voyager 1 Will Break A Cosmic Record, Reaching 1 Light-Day From Earth On November 18, 2026  IFLScience
  3. A probe is about to enter a realm humanity has only imagined until now  Futura, le média qui explore le monde
  4. NASA’s Voyager 1 probe is about to reach a distance once thought unimaginable in space  MSN

item.title: HD 189733b looks deep blue from space, almost Earth-like at first glance — but its colour comes not from oceans, but from silicate particles in a scorching atmosphere where glass may rain sideways through winds of thousands of kilometres an hour. - Space Daily
item.description:
1. HD 189733b looks deep blue from space, almost Earth-like at first glance — but its colour comes not from oceans, but from silicate particles in a scorching atmosphere where glass may rain sideways through winds of thousands of kilometres an hour.  Space Daily
  2. Cotton-candy planets and metal rain: NASA finds some of the strangest worlds in space  The Times of India

item.title: About 66 million years ago an asteroid roughly 10-15 km wide struck off Mexico's Yucatan, gouging a crater some 180 km across — and the extinction that followed wiped out the non-avian dinosaurs and around 75% of all species - Space Daily
item.description:
1. About 66 million years ago an asteroid roughly 10-15 km wide struck off Mexico's Yucatan, gouging a crater some 180 km across — and the extinction that followed wiped out the non-avian dinosaurs and around 75% of all species  Space Daily
  2. Were dinosaurs doomed before the asteroid hit?  Sciworthy

item.title: In the weightlessness of orbit, an astronaut’s heart can become more spherical as it no longer works against gravity in the usual way, while their spine stretches enough to make them measurably taller before they return to Earth. - Space Daily
item.description: In the weightlessness of orbit, an astronaut’s heart can become more spherical as it no longer works against gravity in the usual way, while their spine stretches enough to make them measurably taller before they return to Earth.  Space Daily

item.title: A gem found in a Mars meteorite has scientists puzzled - Yahoo
item.description:

  2. Mineral garnet discovered in Mars meteorite may reveal how the red planet evolved billions of years ago  Phys.org
  3. Scientists discover new rock type on Mars  University of Portsmouth
  4. The Ancient Egyptians loved this rare gem – scientists just found it in a cracked meteorite that fell from Mars  BBC Sky at Night Magazine
  5. Scientists Cracked Open a Mars Meteorite And Found a Big Surprise  ScienceAlert

item.title: The reason no human being has walked on the Moon in more than half a century is not that humanity lost the capability to get there — it is that humanity lost the reason, in the moment the Cold War goal of beating the Soviet Union to the lunar surface had finall - Space Daily

item.title: The tallest volcano in the solar system is so wide that from most places on its slopes, you would never see a dramatic summit rising ahead of you — Olympus Mons is broader than some countries, and its gentle flanks curve away with Mars long before the mou - Space Daily

item.title: NASA's Parker Solar Probe flew in and out of the solar corona and found a source of high-energy particles that no existing model had predicted - Space Daily
item.description:

item.title: In September 2017, after 20 years in space, NASA deliberately flew the Cassini spacecraft into Saturn and let it burn up like a meteor — not because it had failed, but because it had found a possibly habitable ocean on the moon Enceladus that a dead spacecr - 19FortyFive
